en_title: In France, the Council of State considers several bird hunting techniques "illegal"
en_content: After in June, the Council of State ruled, Friday, 6 August, "illegal" several traditional bird hunting techniques, techniques used in the Ardennes and south-western France.Sisi by the associations the League for the Protection of Birds (LPO) and One Voice, "the Council of State today cancels several authorisations to hunt the buffalo, golden plover, larks of the fields, grives and black merles using nets (pants, tendries) or cages (matoles)" because these authorizations "do not comply with the requirements of European law relating to the protection of birds", according to a communiqué. The 2009 European Birds Directive prohibits techniques for the mass capture of birds without distinction of species. A derogation is possible "provided that there is a proper justification and there is no "satisfactory alternative" for the capture of certain birds", recalls the Council of State."The ministerial authorizations in question (...) are not duly substantiated" and the Ministry has not demonstrated that these traditional hunting methods "are the only ones that allow for the capture of buffalo, golden plover, field larks, grives and black whiting". "The only reason to preserve these so-called 'traditional' hunting methods is not enough to allow them," states the Council of State. The Council of State considers that several traditional bird hunting techniques are illegal Pantes, tendries, matoles are no longer allowed to hunt:- buffalo valves- golden plover- field larks- grive- black merles One Voice immediately hailed "a historic victory in line with the one taken against the glut hunters". This technique of trapping with glue-coated stems was banned in June by the court, which after years of rebounding, found that it endangers birds other than those targeted by this hunt, the grives and the merles.VICTORY IMMENCE FOR BIRDSFollowing our 19 appeals, it cancels all orders made between 2018 and 2020 that condemned more than 100,000 birds a year, not counting those killed accidentally. The LPO, for its part, "is now asking the government to act accordingly and to abolish these practices of another age.""If one could conceive the use of traditional hunting in times of famine or war, it is now only a mere leisure", says its president Allain Bougrain-Dubourg, recalling that because of human activities in France. Scientists again fired the alarm bell at the end of May, calling for faster and stronger action to change agricultural practices, particularly related to pesticides, to abandon certain hunting or to rethink the renovation of buildings. The National Federation of Hunters (FNC) denounces "an unbearable decision" "without any serious basis". "She appeals to Prime Minister Jean Castex" and will "consider all possible legal remedies". "For us, traditional hunting is the very essence of hunting passion and will always be at the heart of defending our cynegetic practices," said President Willy Schraen, quoted in the press release. In June, the Ministry of Ecological Transition submitted to the public consultation of the decrees for the 2021-2022 season concerning 106,500 field owls, 5,800 grives and black merles, 1,200 owls and 30 golden plovers, denounces the LPO. One Voice and the LPO say they are ready to attack these new orders. The Ministry of Ecological Transition takes "act of this decision of the Council of State which cancels several ministerial decrees last year authorizing so-called traditional hunting". "This year's decrees, which are being consulted, are being withdrawn", explains the ministry at the AFP, adding that he will "take stock of this matter by analysing the precise decision of the Council of State".France 24 proposes to review the news that marked the weekTake international news everywhere with you! title: En images : à travers le monde, les grandes villes se végétalisent
subtitle: Jardins sur les toits, immeubles végétalisés, arbres plantés dans le désert... Les grandes villes du monde entier rivalisent d'idées pour faire entrer la nature dans les zones urbaines, responsables de fortes émissions de gaz à effet de serre. Tour d'horizon de plusieurs initiatives, de Milan à Singapour en passant par Chengdu.
content: En hauteur sur un toit de New York ou un immeuble de Milan, au ras du sol dans le désert à Riyad ou dans les rues de Medellin : .Faire revenir la nature dans la cité : aujourd'hui, tout le monde est d'accord alors que 70 % des gaz à effet de serre (GES) proviennent des zones urbaines, que les canicules se multiplient de Moscou à Vancouver, que les alertes pollution se répètent de Paris à New Delhi.Quel que soit le rythme de réduction des émissions de GES, les effets dévastateurs du réchauffement sur la nature et les êtres vivants vont s'accélérer et devenir douloureusement palpables bien avant 2050, selon .Dans un élan mondial, urbanistes, architectes, paysagistes, poussés par un public en quête de bien-être et par des élus en recherche d'attractivité, font assaut de projets, de simples bacs fleuris sur les trottoirs aux immeubles les plus fous. La végétalisation des villes est une idée assez nouvelle, soulignent les experts.Le modèle urbain a commencé à changer à la fin des années 1990 à l'ère du développement durable, loin de l'urbanisation massive des années d'après-guerre et leur lot de logements fonctionnalistes, zones commerciales, ghettos urbains et voitures reines. Il s'est développé au début du XXI siècle, à l'ère du réchauffement climatique, au rythme des rapports alarmants du Groupe d'experts intergouvernemental sur l'évolution du climat (Giec).Grâce aux murs et toits végétalisés, la température dans des rues canyons pendant le mois le plus chaud dans neuf villes dans le monde peut être diminuée de 3,6 à 11,3 °C à l'heure la plus chaude, selon le rapport "Nature en ville" de l'Agence française de la transition écologique. Mais tous les projets se valent-ils ?"Pour qu'un projet soit vertueux, il doit répondre à un maximum de fonctions", faire baisser la température de plusieurs degrés, préserver la biodiversité, améliorer le bien-être des habitants, générer une prise de conscience, note Jean Haentjens, coauteur du livre "Éco-urbanisme".Il doit également "répondre au désir du public, être pensé en fonction de l'environnement, adapté au contexte social, offrir une proximité" aux riverains, poursuit l'éco-urbaniste.Arbres géants en béton végétalisés, jardin féérique et montagne sous serre : le site Gardens by the Bay est l'emblème de Singapour, l'attraction de la ville-État près du nouveau quartier d'affaires.Ses 18 impressionnants "Super Trees", recouverts de végétation luxuriante, atteignent 25 à 50 mètres de haut, avec à leur tête des panneaux solaires.Sous ses immenses serres faites d'acier et de verre, le Flower Dome abrite un jardin botanique coloré de dizaines de milliers d'espèces de plantes rares des cinq continents et le Cloud Forest, une montagne artificielle avec sa cascade et ses plantes qui poussent d'habitude à 2 000 m d'altitude. Le lieu, ouvert en 2011 sur 101 hectares, a gagné l'année suivante le prix World Building of the Year.Mais en termes d'intérêt environnemental, les experts restent dubitatifs. "Pourquoi faire des arbres en béton quand on peut en avoir de vrais ?", s'interroge l'auteur du livre "Habiter le monde", Philippe Simay. "Ce sont de supers-objets (...) sans intérêt écologique, une disneylisation de la nature.""Un coup de com'", abonde Claire Doussard, enseignante aménagement et chercheuse associée au Centre national français de la recherche scientifique (CNRS). "On a démontré un savoir-faire technique qui contribue au rayonnement de Singapour" mais, dit-elle, cela contribue aussi à "la sensibilisation du public" qui vient visiter en masse cette nature menacée par le changement climatique.Au milieu des buildings, de hautes cheminées, de routes à quatre voies en bas, statue de la Liberté au loin, des champs de radis, de poireaux, de salades sur un terrain de plus de 14 000 m. Nous sommes sur l'un des plus grands toits cultivés au monde, au 9 étage d'un building de Sunset Park à New York, à la Brooklyn Grange. Ici, on cultive, on vend, on achète, on se balade, on aide les citadins à "retrouver une connexion à la nature".Et ce faisant, on fait baisser la chaleur, améliore la qualité de l'air, enrichit la biodiversité, expliquent les fondateurs de ce projet pionnier.Lancé il y a une dizaine d'années, celui-ci se décline aujourd'hui sur trois toits new-yorkais d'une superficie totale de plus de 22 000 m où sont cultivés plus de 45 tonnes de produits bio par an.D'une manière générale, l'aspect logistique est lourd pour ce type de culture, note Claire Doussard, directrice de la publication du livre "(re)Penser la ville du XXI siècle". Il faut isoler, monter la terre, l'eau, descendre les salades... "Ces fermes doivent être rentables car il y a beaucoup de contraintes", note-t-elle.Deux hectares de forêt en hauteur ainsi que 20 000 plantes et arbres sont répartis sur deux immeubles au beau milieu du quartier milanais de Porta Nuova. On l'appelle le "Bosco Verticale" ("forêt verticale" en français).Sur chaque balcon poussent des dizaines de plantes ou d'arbres, choisis et positionnés en fonction de leur résistance au vent et de leurs préférences en matière de luminosité ou d'humidité.Ce projet est "né de mon obsession pour les arbres" et d'une réflexion sur "la manière dont ils pouvaient devenir une composante essentielle de l'architecture", expliquait en 2017 à l'AFP son architecte, Stefano Boeri. L'idée était aussi de construire un édifice qui puisse "contribuer à réduire la pollution de la ville".Terminé en 2014, le "Bosco" a reçu en 2015 le titre de l'immeuble le plus beau et le plus innovant au monde par le Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat (Chicago).Mais on le qualifie aussi de "bling bling", avec ses appartements qui peuvent atteindre les 15 000 euros le m2, de "gadget" et de "peu vertueux".À Copenhague, la "Ferme verticale" fait pousser des salades et des herbes au rythme de 15 récoltes par an, à la lumière artificielle uniquement. Quelque 7 000 m remplis d'étagères hautes de 10 mètres sur 14 niveaux, éclairées par un total de 20 000 ampoules LED.Ici, les semences sont transportées par des robots qui roulent entre les allées et on jardine du haut d'un monte-charge. Sise dans un hangar de Taastrup dans la zone industrielle de Copenhague, cette ferme urbaine a été inaugurée en décembre par la start-up danoise Nordic Harvest."Nous prouvons qu'il est possible de déplacer une partie de la production agricole dans les villes", dit son PDG, Anders Riemann.C'est le projet "high tech" par excellence, note Philippe Simay. "Il coûte très cher en entretien, en main d'œuvre qualifiée, il faut des lumières allumées 24 heures sur 24. On est dans le fantasme de la production avec des gens qui disent 'on va nourrir les villes'", pointe le spécialiste. Aujourd'hui, on ne voit que des pousses perdues au milieu d'autoroutes à voies multiples et de gigantesques échangeurs. Au loin des dunes. Dans l'air, pollution, poussière et chaleur.Mais d'ici 2030, le projet "Green Riyadh" prévoit de planter 7,5 millions d'arbres et de créer 3 000 parcs urbains pour faire de la capitale saoudienne une ville plus verte.Un projet à 11 milliards de dollars qui nécessitera un million de m d'eau chaque jour, recyclé par le biais d'un système d'irrigation souterrain réutilisant les eaux usées, promet-on."Réintroduire des arbres dans le désert, c'est très vertueux, on gagne en rafraîchissement", estime Cédissia About. "La question sera de savoir s'ils utilisent des produits phyto qui font fuir oiseaux et insectes alors que l'enjeu prioritaire, c'est d'accroître la biodiversité."Les voies urbaines, auparavant privées de nature, ont été transformées en 30 "corridors verts" arborés et fleuris, "connectés" en réseau aux espaces verts déjà existants, des parcs ou pelouses publics aux jardins privés.Une continuité verte qui a permis à Medellin, deuxième ville de Colombie, de faire baisser la température de deux degrés, d'aider à purifier l'air, de faire revenir abeilles et oiseaux, d'impliquer les habitants, de créer des emplois de jardiniers, vante une vidéo de la municipalité qui porte le projet depuis 2016.Le projet a été multiprimé – il a reçu en 2019 le Aschden Award et le C40 Cities Bloomberg Philanthropies Award – pour avoir "amélioré la biodiversité", "réduit la chaleur", "contribué au bien-être des citoyens", selon le site C40 Cities."C'est un des meilleurs exemples (de végétalisation), porté par une politique, qui accroît la biodiversité, avec une dimension sociale", commente Philippe Simay."C'est mieux quand on réfléchit à grande échelle en termes de continuité écologique, cela a un vrai impact", confirme Claire Doussard. "Il y a eu une vraie réflexion à l'échelle de la ville, sur les espèces choisies, l'habitabilité, les contraintes."À Chengdu, en Chine, huit bâtiments ont été végétalisés grâce à des plantes qui poussent sur les terrasses d'appartements dans un quartier résidentiel de la capitale du Sichuan. Ce projet expérimental a été lancé en 2018 et baptisé "Jardin forestier de Qiyi". Par endroits, on y vit comme dans une cabane haut perchée dans un arbre, au milieu de la forêt tropicale et du gazouillis des oiseaux. La qualité de "l'air est bonne quand on se réveille le matin et voir ces arbres pleins de verdure nous fait du bien à nous, personnes âgées", affirme un habitant, Lin Dengying.Ailleurs, la nature semble avoir repris ses droits, la jungle a envahi les balcons, étalée sur les murs, affalée d'un étage à l'autre. En septembre, le quotidien semi-officiel Global Times évoquait un faible taux d'occupation des 826 appartements, qui selon des locataires serait dû à la présence de nombreux moustiques.Pour ce type de projet, il est certes important de penser à l'impact environnemental mais il faut prendre en considération "l'entretien" et "l'habitabilité", note Claire Doussard.France 24 vous propose de revenir sur les actualités qui ont marqué la semaineEmportez l'actualité internationale partout avec vous ! en_title: Images: cities around the world vegetate
en_content: Up on a roof in New York or a building in Milan, on the ground in the desert in Riyadh or in the streets of Medellin : .Make nature come back to the city : today, everyone agrees that 70% of the greenhouse gases (GHGs) come from urban areas, that the heat waves are multiplying from Moscow to Vancouver, that the pollution alerts are repeated from Paris to New Delhi.Whatever the rate of reduction of GHG emissions, the devastating effects of warming on nature and living beings will accelerate and become painfully palpable long before 2050, according to .In a global momentum, urbanists, architects, landscapers, pushed by an audience in search of well-being and by elected officials in search of attractiveness, storm projects, simple flower bins on the sidewalks to the craziest buildings. The vegetation of cities is a fairly new idea, the experts point out. The urban model began to change in the late 1990s in the era of sustainable development, far from the massive urbanization of the post-war years and their lot of functional housing, commercial areas, urban ghettos and queen cars. It developed at the beginning of the 21st century, in the era of global warming, at the pace of the alarming reports of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IGC). Thanks to the vegetated walls and roofs, the temperature in canyon streets during the warmest month in nine cities in the world can be reduced from 3.6 to 11.3 °C at the warmest time, according to the report "Nature en ville" of the French Agency for the Ecological Transition. But are all projects worth it?"For a project to be virtuous, it must respond to as many functions as possible," said Jean Haentjens, co-author of the book "Eco-urbanism". It must also "respond to the desire of the public, be thought according to the environment, adapted to the social context, offer a proximity" to the residents, continues the eco-urbanist. Giant trees in vegetated concrete, fairy garden and mountain under greenhouse: Gardens by the Bay is the emblem of Singapore, the attraction of the city-state near the new business district. Its 18 impressive "Super Trees", covered with lush vegetation, reach 25 to 50 meters high, with solar panels at their head. Under its huge glass and steel greenhouses, the Flower Dome houses a colourful botanical garden of tens of thousands of rare plant species from five continents and the Cloud Forest, an artificial mountain with its waterfall and plants that usually grow at 2,000 m above sea level. The venue, opened in 2011 on 101 hectares, won the World Building of the Year award the following year, but in terms of environmental interest, experts remain dubious. "Why make concrete trees when you can have real ones?" asked the author of the book "Habiter le monde", Philippe Simay. "These are super-objects (...) without ecological interest, a disneylisation of nature.""A coup de com'", abounds Claire Doussard, teacher development and researcher associated with the French National Centre for Scientific Research (CNRS). "Technical know-how has been demonstrated that contributes to Singapore's outreach," she says, "but it also contributes to "public awareness" that comes to visit this nature in mass, threatened by climate change. In the middle of buildings, high chimneys, roads with four lanes down, statue of Liberty in the distance, fields of radishes, leeks, salads on a plot of more than 14,000 m. We are on one of the largest cultivated roofs in the world, on the 9th floor of a Sunset Park building in New York, Brooklyn Grange. Here we grow, sell, buy, walk, help urban dwellers to "find a connection to nature". In doing so, heat is lowered, air quality is improved, biodiversity is enhanced, and the founders of this pioneering project explain. Launched a decade ago, the project is now on three New York roofs with a total area of more than 22,000 m where more than 45 tons of organic products are grown per year. In general, the logistical aspect is heavy for this type of culture, notes Claire Doussard, director of the publication of the book "(re)Thinking the City of the XXI Century". We have to isolate the land, the water, the salads... "These farms must be profitable because there are a lot of constraints," she notes.Two hectares of high forest as well as 20,000 plants and trees are spread over two buildings in the middle of the Milanese district of Porta Nuova. It is called the "Bosco Verticale" ("vertical forest" in French). On each balcony dozens of plants or trees grow, chosen and positioned according to their wind resistance and their preferences for brightness or humidity. This project is "born from my obsession with trees" and a reflection on "how they could become an essential component of architecture", explained in 2017 to AFP its architect, Stefano Boeri. The idea was also to build a building that could "contribute to reducing the city's pollution".Deleted in 2014, the "Bosco" received in 2015 the title of the most beautiful and innovative building in the world by the Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat (Chicago).But it is also called a "bling bling", with its apartments that can reach 15,000 euros a m2, "gadget" and "poor virtuous".In Copenhagen, the "vertical Ferm" grows salads and herbs at the rate of 15 harvests per year, with artificial light only. Some 7,000 m filled with 10 meters high shelves on 14 levels, illuminated by a total of 20,000 LED bulbs.Here, the seeds are transported by robots running between the alleys and gardening from the top of a lift. Located in a Taastrup shed in the industrial area of Copenhagen, this urban farm was inaugurated in December by the Danish start-up Nordic Harvest."We prove that it is possible to move part of the agricultural production in the cities," said its CEO, Anders Riemann.This is the "high tech" project par excellence, notes Philippe Simay. "It costs a lot in maintenance, in skilled labour, it takes lights lit 24 hours a day. We are in the fantasy of production with people who say 'we're going to feed the cities'," the specialist points out. Today, we only see shoots lost in the middle of multi-lane highways and gigantic exchangers. Away from the dunes. In the air, pollution, dust and heat.But by 2030, the Green Riyadh project plans to plant 7.5 million trees and create 3,000 urban parks to make the Saudi capital a greener city. A $11 billion project that will require a million m of water every day, recycled through an underground irrigation system that reuses wastewater, it is promised."Reintroduction of trees into the desert is very virtuous, we gain refreshment," Cédissia About says. "The question will be whether they use plant products that cause birds and insects to flee, while the priority is to increase biodiversity. "Urban roads, previously deprived of nature, have been transformed into 30 "green corridors" with trees and flowers, "connected" with existing green spaces, from public parks or lawns to private gardens. A green continuity that allowed Medellin, Colombia's second-largest city, to lower the temperature by two degrees, help purify the air, bring bees and birds back, involve the inhabitants, create jobs for gardeners, boasts a video of the municipality that has been carrying the project since 2016. The project was awarded the Aschden Award and the C40 Cities Bloomberg Philanthropies Award in 2019 for "improved biodiversity," "reduces heat," "contributed to the well-being of citizens," according to C40 Cities."This is one of the best examples (vegetation), driven by a policy that increases biodiversity, with a social dimension," says Philippe Simay."It's better when you think on a large scale in terms of ecological continuity, it has a real impact," says Claire Doussard. "There was a real reflection on the scale of the city, on the chosen species, habitability, constraints."In Chengdu, China, eight buildings were vegetated thanks to plants growing on the apartment terraces in a residential area of the capital of Sichuan. This experimental project was launched in 2018 and called "Qiyi Forest Garden". In places, they live as in a high cabin perched in a tree, in the middle of the tropical forest and the birds' gazewellies. The quality of "the air is good when you wake up in the morning and see these trees full of greenery is good for us, the elderly", says Lin Dengying, a resident. Elsewhere, nature seems to have taken over its rights, the jungle has invaded the balconies, spread over the walls, collapsed from one floor to the other. In September, the semi-official newspaper Global Times reported a low occupancy rate of 826 apartments, which according to tenants is due to the presence of many mosquitoes. For this type of project, it is important to think about the environmental impact, but we must take into account "maintenance" and "habitatability", notes Claire Doussard.France 24 proposes to come back to the news that marked the weekTake international news everywhere with you! en_subtitle: Gardens on roofs, vegetated buildings, trees planted in the desert... Large cities around the world compete with ideas to bring nature into urban areas, which are responsible for high greenhouse gas emissions. Overview of several initiatives, from Milan to Singapore to Chengdu.

On each balcony dozens of plants or trees grow, chosen and positioned according to their wind resistance and their preferences for brightness or humidity. This project is "born from my obsession with trees" and a reflection on "how they could become an essential component of architecture", explained in 2017 to AFP its architect, Stefano Boeri. The idea was also to build a building that could "contribute to reducing the city's pollution".Deleted in 2014, the "Bosco" received in 2015 the title of the most beautiful and innovative building in the world by the Council on Tall Buildings and Urban Habitat (Chicago).But it is also called a "bling bling", with its apartments that can reach 15,000 euros a m2, "gadget" and "poor virtuous".In Copenhagen, the "vertical Ferm" grows salads and herbs at the rate of 15 harvests per year, with artificial light only. Some 7,000 m filled with 10 meters high shelves on 14 levels, illuminated by a total of 20,000 LED bulbs.Here, the seeds are transported by robots running between the alleys and gardening from the top of a lift. Located in a Taastrup shed in the industrial area of Copenhagen, this urban farm was inaugurated in December by the Danish start-up Nordic Harvest."We prove that it is possible to move part of the agricultural production in the cities," said its CEO, Anders Riemann.This is the "high tech" project par excellence, notes Philippe Simay. "It costs a lot in maintenance, in skilled labour, it takes lights lit 24 hours a day. We are in the fantasy of production with people who say 'we're going to feed the cities'," the specialist points out. Today, we only see shoots lost in the middle of multi-lane highways and gigantic exchangers. Away from the dunes. In the air, pollution, dust and heat.But by 2030, the Green Riyadh project plans to plant 7.5 million trees and create 3,000 urban parks to make the Saudi capital a greener city. A $11 billion project that will require a million m of water every day, recycled through an underground irrigation system that reuses wastewater, it is promised."Reintroduction of trees into the desert is very virtuous, we gain refreshment," Cédissia About says. "The question will be whether they use plant products that cause birds and insects to flee, while the priority is to increase biodiversity. "Urban roads, previously deprived of nature, have been transformed into 30 "green corridors" with trees and flowers, "connected" with existing green spaces, from public parks or lawns to private gardens. A green continuity that allowed Medellin, Colombia's second-largest city, to lower the temperature by two degrees, help purify the air, bring bees and birds back, involve the inhabitants, create jobs for gardeners, boasts a video of the municipality that has been carrying the project since 2016. The project was awarded the Aschden Award and the C40 Cities Bloomberg Philanthropies Award in 2019 for "improved biodiversity," "reduces heat," "contributed to the well-being of citizens," according to C40 Cities."This is one of the best examples (vegetation), driven by a policy that increases biodiversity, with a social dimension," says Philippe Simay."It's better when you think on a large scale in terms of ecological continuity, it has a real impact," says Claire Doussard. "There was a real reflection on the scale of the city, on the chosen species, habitability, constraints."In Chengdu, China, eight buildings were vegetated thanks to plants growing on the apartment terraces in a residential area of the capital of Sichuan. This experimental project was launched in 2018 and called "Qiyi Forest Garden". In places, they live as in a high cabin perched in a tree, in the middle of the tropical forest and the birds' gazewellies. The quality of "the air is good when you wake up in the morning and see these trees full of greenery is good for us, the elderly", says Lin Dengying, a resident. Elsewhere, nature seems to have taken over its rights, the jungle has invaded the balconies, spread over the walls, collapsed from one floor to the other. In September, the semi-official newspaper Global Times reported a low occupancy rate of 826 apartments, which according to tenants is due to the presence of many mosquitoes. For this type of project, it is important to think about the environmental impact, but we must take into account "maintenance" and "habitatability", notes Claire Doussard.France 24 proposes to come back to the news that marked the weekTake international news everywhere with you! subtitle_nlp.text: Gardens on roofs, vegetated buildings, trees planted in the desert... Large cities around the world compete with ideas to bring nature into urban areas, which are responsible for high greenhouse gas emissions. Overview of several initiatives, from Milan to Singapore to Chengdu.

title: Naissance de deux lynx boréal dans l'est de la France, une première depuis le 17e siècle
subtitle: Lycka, une femelle lynx boréal introduite en mars 2020 dans le Palatinat en Allemagne dans le cadre d'un programme de réintroduction, a mis au monde deux petits dans les Vosges du Nord dans l'est de la France. C’est la première reproduction attestée de l’espèce depuis sa disparition du massif au 17e siècle.
content: Une naissance historique pour une espèce menacée. Deux chatons lynx boréal sont nés au printemps dans le massif des Vosges du nord, dans l'est de la France, a annoncé, vendredi 6 août,  (OFB). Une première depuis la disparition du félin de la région au 17 siècle.🐱  : une première portée de  boréal observée dans les Vosges du Nord depuis le 17e siècle🐾 Le suivi, assuré par l’, a permis de confirmer que la femelle dénommée Lycka a donné naissance ce printemps à 2 chatons👉  La mère, Lycka, une femelle âgée de 10 ans, avait été relâchée en mars 2020 dans le massif frontalier du Palatinat en Allemagne dans le cadre d'un programme européen de réintroduction, a précisé dans un communiqué l'OFB, qui suit les mouvements de l'animal grâce à un collier GPS.Née en Suisse, Lycka a vécu dans le canton de Neuchâtel avant d'être transférée le 20 mars 2020 dans la forêt du Palatinat. Elle est arrivée fin mars 2020 sur le territoire français, selon les relevés des équipes d'observation française et allemande."C'est la coopération internationale en faveur de la biodiversité qui porte ses fruits", a salué l'OFB, qui souligne que la naissance de cette première portée depuis le retour du lynx dans les Vosges représente "une nouvelle porteuse d'espoir pour l'espèce", même si son statut de conservation demeure "très précaire".Reconnaissable à son pelage beige et à ses grandes oreilles pointues, le lynx boréal est le seul grand félin sauvage présent en France, et est classé comme espèce "en danger" sur la  (UICN).Les deux tiers de la population de lynx en France, qui s'élève à 150 individus environ, sont situés dans le massif du Jura. L'animal est également présent dans l'est, dans les Alpes et, de manière sporadique, dans le massif des Vosges. À peine une petite dizaine d’individus sont recensés actuellement sur l’ensemble du massif, Lycka étant la seule femelle connue.Le lynx avait disparu du pays au début du XX siècle, avant d'être réintroduit dans l'est de la France dans les années 80 et 90. Les services de l'État préparent depuis 2019 un Plan national d'actions qui a pour objectif de "rétablir la population française de lynx boréal dans un état de conservation favorable" et "la lutte contre les destructions illégales est une de ses priorités", selon la Direction de l'environnement.France 24 vous propose de revenir sur les actualités qui ont marqué la semaineEmportez l'actualité internationale partout avec vous ! en_title: Birth of two boreal lynx in eastern France, a first since the 17th century
en_content: A historical birth for a threatened species. Two boreal lynx kittens were born in the spring in the Vosges massif of the north, in the east of France, announced on Friday 6 August (OFB). A first since the disappearance of the feline of the region in the 17th century. The follow-up, carried out by the Is, confirmed that the female named Lycka gave birth this spring to 2 kittens The mother, Lycka, a 10-year-old female, had been released in March 2020 in the border massif of the Palatinate in Germany as part of a European reintroduction programme, said in a press release the OFB, which follows the movements of the animal thanks to a GPS necklace.Born in Switzerland, Lycka lived in the canton of Neuchâtel before being transferred on 20 March 2020 to the Palatinate forest. It arrived at the end of March 2020 on French territory, according to the surveys of the French and German observation teams."It is the international cooperation in favour of biodiversity that bears fruit", the OFB welcomed, which stresses that the birth of this first litter since the return of the lynx to the Vosges represents "a new carrier of hope for the species", even though its conservation status remains "very precarious". Recognising its beige coat and its large pointed ears, the boreal lynx is the only large wild feline present in France, and is classified as "in danger" on the (IUCN). Two thirds of the lynx population in France, which amounts to about 150 individuals, are located in the Jura massif. The animal is also present in the east, in the Alps and sporadically in the Vosges massif. Only a few dozen individuals are currently recorded throughout the massif, with Lycka being the only female known. The lynx had disappeared from the country at the beginning of the XX century, before being reintroduced into eastern France in the 1980s and 1990s. The State departments have been preparing since 2019 a National Action Plan which aims to "reestablish the French population of boreal lynx in a favourable state of conservation" and "the fight against illegal destruction is one of its priorities", according to the Environmental Directorate.France 24 proposes to review the news that marked the weekTake international news with you everywhere! It is the first attested reproduction of the species since its disappearance from the massif in the 17th century.

title: Challenge App Afrique 2024 : qui sont les trois finalistes invités à Abidjan ?
subtitle: Les membres du jury du Challenge App Afrique ont sélectionné les trois finalistes de la quatrième édition parmi 800 candidatures reçues. Ils sont originaires du Burkina Faso, du Maroc et du Congo.
content: Cette année, le concours vise à encourager l'émergence de solutions numériques favorisant les énergies vertes en . Accessible à tous les jeunes entrepreneurs africains francophones, cette édition récompensera les innovations numériques telles que les sites Internet et les applications, qui contribuent à améliorer l'accès aux Les trois finalistes, qui auront l'opportunité de participer à la finale du Challenge App Afrique le 3 avril prochain à l'Institut Français de Côte d'Ivoire à Abidjan sont :•  : application facilitant l'utilisation des bornes de recharge électrique solaire au Burkina Faso en permettant aux utilisateurs de localiser et de vérifier la disponibilité en temps réel des bornes, tout en effectuant le paiement directement depuis l'application. : une plateforme d'Energy Management System pour les smart grids (réseaux électriques "intelligents") optimisant la production, le stockage et la consommation d'énergie, rendant l'énergie accessible en milieu rural africain. : ™️ est une application de gestion agricole surveillant les produits entrant et sortant du système, offrant des alertes sur la qualité des produits et leur conservation, ainsi que des prévisions de prix en temps réel pour aider les agriculteurs à évaluer la valeur de leurs produits sur le marché.Le lauréat, qui se verra financer le développement de son projet à hauteur de 15 000 euros, sera dévoilé le 3 avril prochain lors de l’enregistrement en public de l’émission "À travers ce concours, RFI et France 24 souhaitent encourager et soutenir l’innovation, en stimulant et en accompagnant les jeunes entrepreneurs africains.Le jury de cette première sélection est composé de Anne-Cécile Bras, journaliste à RFI et présentatrice de l’émission "C’est pas du vent" ; Guillaume Grallet, journaliste au Point et chroniqueur de  sur France 24 ; Jeanne Richard, journaliste pour Questions d’Environnement ; Aphrodice Mutangana, directeur des partenariats chez Digital Africa ; Arona Diedhiou, directeur de recherche à l’Institut de recherche pour le développement (IRD) ;  Olivia Hervy, cheffe de l'Écosystème chez Viva Technology ; Olivier Prentout, directeur marketing au sein d’Ayoba ; Philippe Miquel, directeur général CFAO Infrastructure – secteur B2B ; Karim Sy, fondateur de l'association Jokkolabs ; et enfin Armand Sofack, fondateur et CEO de Mobility Cloud.Lancée par RFI et France 24 et ses partenaires CFAO Infrastructure, Digital Africa, IRD, Ayoba, Viva Technology, le Club Abidjan Ville Durable, l’AFD, Digital Energy Facility (financé par l’Union européenne et mis en œuvre par l’AFD), l’Ademe et Ecobank. La huitième édition du Challenge App Afrique a pour objectif de récompenser les innovations numériques permettant d’améliorer l’énergie verte en Afrique.France 24 vous propose de revenir sur les actualités qui ont marqué la semaineEmportez l'actualité internationale partout avec vous ! en_title: Challenge App Africa 2024: Who are the three finalists invited to Abidjan?
en_content: This year, the competition aims to encourage the emergence of digital solutions that promote green energy in Europe. Accessible to all young French-speaking African entrepreneurs, this edition will reward digital innovations such as websites and applications, which help improve access to the Three Finalists, who will have the opportunity to participate in the final of the Challenge App Africa on April 3rd at the Institut Français de Côte d'Ivoire in Abidjan are:•: application facilitating the use of solar electric charging terminals in Burkina Faso by allowing users to locate and verify the availability of terminals in real time, while making the payment directly from the application. : a platform of Energy Management System for smart grids (intelligent electrical grids) optimizing energy production, storage and consumption, making energy available in rural Africa. : TM The winner, who will receive funding for the development of his project to the tune of 15,000 euros, will be unveiled on 3 April during the public registration of the program "Through this contest, RFI and France 24 want to encourage and support innovation, by stimulating and accompanying young African entrepreneurs. The jury for this first selection is composed of Anne-Cécile Bras, a journalist at RFI and presenter of the show "C Guillaume Grallet, journalist and columnist of France 24 ; Jeanne Richard, Journalist for Environment Issues; Aphrodice Mutangana, Director of Partnerships at Digital Africa; Arona Diedhiou, Director of Research at the Institute for Development Research (IRD); Olivia Hervy, Head of the Ecosystem at Viva Technology; Olivier Prentout, Marketing Director at Ayoba; Philippe Miquel, Director General CFAO Infrastructure – Sector B2B; Karim Sy, founder of the association Jokkolabs; and finally Armand Sofack, founder and CEO of Mobility Cloud. Launched by RFI and France 24 and its partners CFAO Infrastructure, Digital Africa, IRD, Ayoba, Viva Technology, Club Abidjan Ville Durable, L-AFD, Digital Energy Facility (financed by the European Union and implemented by AFD), Ademe and Ecobank. The eighth edition of the Challenge App Africa aims to reward digital innovations to improve green energy in Africa.France 24 proposes to come back to the news that marked the weekTake international news everywhere with you! cont_nlp.text: This year, the competition aims to encourage the emergence of digital solutions that promote green energy in Europe. Accessible to all young French-speaking African entrepreneurs, this edition will reward digital innovations such as websites and applications, which help improve access to the Three Finalists, who will have the opportunity to participate in the final of the Challenge App Africa on April 3rd at the Institut Français de Côte d'Ivoire in Abidjan are:•: application facilitating the use of solar electric charging terminals in Burkina Faso by allowing users to locate and verify the availability of terminals in real time, while making the payment directly from the application. : a platform of Energy Management System for smart grids (intelligent electrical grids) optimizing energy production, storage and consumption, making energy available in rural Africa. : TM The winner, who will receive funding for the development of his project to the tune of 15,000 euros, will be unveiled on 3 April during the public registration of the program "Through this contest, RFI and France 24 want to encourage and support innovation, by stimulating and accompanying young African entrepreneurs. The jury for this first selection is composed of Anne-Cécile Bras, a journalist at RFI and presenter of the show "C Guillaume Grallet, journalist and columnist of France 24 ; Jeanne Richard, Journalist for Environment Issues; Aphrodice Mutangana, Director of Partnerships at Digital Africa; Arona Diedhiou, Director of Research at the Institute for Development Research (IRD); Olivia Hervy, Head of the Ecosystem at Viva Technology; Olivier Prentout, Marketing Director at Ayoba; Philippe Miquel, Director General CFAO Infrastructure – Sector B2B; Karim Sy, founder of the association Jokkolabs; and finally Armand Sofack, founder and CEO of Mobility Cloud. Launched by RFI and France 24 and its partners CFAO Infrastructure, Digital Africa, IRD, Ayoba, Viva Technology, Club Abidjan Ville Durable, L-AFD, Digital Energy Facility (financed by the European Union and implemented by AFD), Ademe and Ecobank. title_nlp.text: Challenge App Africa 2024: Who are the three finalists invited to Abidjan?

subtitle_nlp.text: The members of the Challenge App Africa jury selected the three finalists for the fourth edition from 800 applications received. They originate in Burkina Faso, Morocco and the Congo.
